topics.title: Standard Card
topics.pageSubtitle: Text + analogy + formula + insight. The basic building block.

cards.title: What is Entropy?
cards.sub: A measure of disorder or uncertainty in a system
cards.body: Entropy measures how unpredictable a system is. In thermodynamics it measures disorder; in information theory it measures the average amount of information in a message.
cards.analogy: A perfectly sorted deck of cards has zero entropy — totally predictable. A shuffled deck has maximum entropy — you cannot predict the next card.
cards.insight: Entropy always increases in an isolated system — this is the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

topics.title: Markdown Card
topics.pageSubtitle: Long-form blog posts, research notes, essays — full rich text.

cards.title: Research Note: Transformer Scaling Laws
cards.content:
Overview

Scaling laws in language models describe how model performance improves predictably with more compute, data, and parameters. The Chinchilla paper (Hoffmann et al., 2022) showed that most large models were significantly undertrained.

Key Finding

For a compute-optimal model, you should scale parameters and tokens equally. Specifically:

  • For every doubling of parameters, double the training tokens
  • GPT-3 (175B params) was trained on ~300B tokens — Chinchilla-optimal would require ~3.5T tokens
  • Llama 2 7B trained on 2T tokens outperforms GPT-3 on most benchmarks
The insight is not just to train bigger models — it is to train smarter, with the right data-to-parameter ratio.

Implications

This finding reshapes how we think about the economics of AI. Smaller, well-trained models can beat larger, undertrained ones at a fraction of the inference cost.

Read the original paper: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models
cards.insight: Takeaway: More tokens, not just more parameters. Chinchilla changed how every major lab trains models.

cards.title: Deriving the Quadratic Formula
cards.body: We start from the general quadratic equation and derive the formula by completing the square.
cards.insight: The discriminant $b^2 - 4ac$ tells everything: positive = 2 real roots, zero = 1 root, negative = complex roots.

cards.title: Bayes' Theorem
cards.sub: Conditional probability — the foundation of probabilistic inference
cards.body: Bayes' theorem describes how to update beliefs given new evidence.
cards.analogy: You have a positive COVID test. Bayes tells you the actual probability of being sick depends on: how accurate the test is (likelihood), how common COVID is right now (prior), and how many people test positive overall (evidence).
cards.insight: Prior × Likelihood → Posterior. Every update in Bayesian ML follows this exact pattern.

topics.title: Diagram Card
topics.pageSubtitle: Flowcharts, decision trees, network graphs — all from text.

cards.title: ML Model Training Pipeline
cards.sub: From raw data to deployed model
cards.body: Every ML system follows this general pipeline regardless of the specific algorithm used.
cards.diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[Raw Data] --> B[Preprocessing]
  B --> C[Feature Engineering]
  C --> D[Train / Val / Test Split]
  D --> E[Model Training]
  E --> F{Validation Score OK?}
  F -- No --> G[Tune Hyperparams]
  G --> E
  F -- Yes --> H[Test Evaluation]
  H --> I[Deploy]
cards.caption: Fig 1. Standard ML pipeline — iterate until validation score is acceptable
cards.insight: The loop between training → validation → tuning is where most of the real work happens.

cards.title: Training vs Validation Loss
cards.body: This graph shows a typical training curve. Training loss decreases continuously, but validation loss starts increasing after epoch 15 — the model is overfitting.
cards.caption: Overfitting begins around epoch 15 — validation loss starts rising while training loss continues falling
cards.insight: The gap between train and val loss is a direct measure of overfitting severity.

cards.title: Gradient Descent Visualization
cards.sub: Watch the ball roll down to the minimum
cards.body: Gradient descent iteratively moves toward the minimum of the loss surface. The learning rate controls how big each step is.
cards.html:
Start
Minimum (loss=0)
cards.caption: Each step moves toward lower loss — step size controlled by learning rate
cards.insight: Too large a step = overshoot and diverge. Too small = takes forever. Adam optimizer adapts the rate automatically.

topics.title: Code Card
topics.pageSubtitle: Syntax-highlighted code with copy button. Any language.

cards.title: Linear Regression from Scratch
cards.body: This implements gradient descent for linear regression manually, showing exactly what happens under the hood of every ML library.
cards.code:
import numpy as np

class LinearRegression:
    def __init__(self, lr=0.01, epochs=1000):
        self.lr = lr
        self.epochs = epochs
        self.weights = None
        self.bias = None

    def fit(self, X, y):
        n_samples, n_features = X.shape
        self.weights = np.zeros(n_features)
        self.bias = 0

        for epoch in range(self.epochs):
            # Forward pass
            y_pred = X @ self.weights + self.bias

            # Compute gradients
            dw = (2 / n_samples) * X.T @ (y_pred - y)
            db = (2 / n_samples) * np.sum(y_pred - y)

            # Update parameters
            self.weights -= self.lr * dw
            self.bias    -= self.lr * db

    def predict(self, X):
        return X @ self.weights + self.bias

# Usage
model = LinearRegression(lr=0.01, epochs=500)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
predictions = model.predict(X_test)
cards.caption: Each forward pass → compute gradients → update weights is exactly one step of gradient descent
cards.insight: dw and db are the gradients of MSE loss with respect to weights and bias.

cards.title: Transformer Attention
cards.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

def scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, mask=None):
    """
    Q, K, V: (batch, heads, seq_len, d_k)
    Returns: attention output + weights
    """
    d_k = Q.size(-1)

    # Compute attention scores
    scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(d_k)

    # Apply mask (for causal / padding)
    if mask is not None:
        scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float('-inf'))

    # Softmax over last dim
    weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)

    # Weighted sum of values
    output = torch.matmul(weights, V)
    return output, weights

cards.title: Backpropagation
cards.sub: The algorithm that trains every neural network
cards.body: Backpropagation computes gradients by applying the chain rule of calculus from the output layer backward through the network.
cards.analogy: Like tracing a mistake backward through an assembly line — each worker (layer) gets a blame score proportional to their contribution to the final error.

cards.title: Chain Rule — The Math Behind Backprop
cards.display: \frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a} \cdot \frac{\partial a}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial w}

steps.label: Propagate backward
steps.note: Each layer gets gradient from the layer ahead

steps.label: Compute weight gradient
steps.note: Outer product of delta and previous activation

cards.insight: The gradient flows backward through layers — that's why it's called backpropagation.

cards.code:
# Simplified backprop for one layer
def backward(dout, cache):
    x, w, b = cache
    dx = dout @ w.T          # gradient w.r.t. input
    dw = x.T @ dout          # gradient w.r.t. weights
    db = dout.sum(axis=0)    # gradient w.r.t. bias
    return dx, dw, db

cards.caption: Loss decreasing over epochs as backprop + gradient descent optimizes the network
